channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: EMPULSE is a Slick, Polished Competitive Shooter With a Hook
item.content:encoded:
The devs at 1047 Games know a thing or two about first-person shooters. Splitgate took off and became a hit among fans, invoking feelings of Halo and Portal in one tight package.

With Splitgate's sequel now finished, 1047 sets its sights on a new project. It's within the same genre, but it will undoubtedly appeal greatly to those with fond memories of, again, Halo, but also Titanfall. This is EMPULSE.

EMPULSE is a competitive 6v6 shooter with a heavy emphasis on slick, smooth parkour movement and tight gunplay. Maps are smaller in scale to incentivize fast-paced firefights, while also enabling players to struggle to gain control over powerful mechs.

Rather than calling down mechs from the sky a la Titanfall, EMPULSE's developers see these machines as something more akin to a rocket launcher pickup from Halo. Everyone's going to want one, and it's a weapon powerful enough to shift the tide of battle.

Jumping into a mech gives players a powerful protective layer of armor, as well as a shield to temporarily soak up damage. The rockets and machine guns on mechs are a force to be reckoned with.

Even so, EMPULSE's developers were sure to provide a proper foil to these tide-turning machines. Loadouts are equipped with melee weapons that do extra damage to mechs, so while it's dangerous to approach them from up close, it's also a good way to end someone's kill streak.

For the most part, though, EMPULSE's core gameplay revolves around its parkour-based movement. Outside of mechs, movement feels very similar to Titanfall, with the ability to scale ledges with ease, run on walls, and more. The map design takes advantage of these mechanics with various pieces of architecture and jump pads.

There's also a grappling hook. I mean, every good FPS has a grappling hook, and that just adds to the movement and momentum of EMPULSE's gameplay. 1047 Games CEO Ian Proulx showed me a neat trick with the grappling hook: by aiming the cursor at the point of grapple, you can turn corners with ease.

He also grappled to a tree and was able to swing around and around in a circle. While this might not have too many practical gameplay benefits, it's definitely showing the potential of EMPULSE's movement system.

Beyond that, gunplay is very tight, and weapons feel impactful. The time to kill is shorter, and it's a game that definitely requires a mouse for accuracy. Outside of this, grenades are another useful tool, but these act a bit differently than other FPSs.

These are P.A.I.N.T. grenades, which, well, "paint" an area with their effects. This could be a simple explosion, except the radius is more of a paint burst than anything else. More interestingly, these grenades can act as a utility as well.

There's one that paints an area with an effect that boosts your jump, while another is sticky and slows players down. These were the ones I tried, but there's definitely more and it's a cool take on the typical grenade system.

I'm not the best at competitive shooters anymore; if there ever was a time I was good at them, it's long past. Even so, I feel like I have enough experience in the genre to know when a game is exciting and well-designed, and EMPULSE is definitely one of those.

EMPULSE was played during Summer Game Fest Play Days 2026.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/empulse-is-slick-polished-competitive-shooter-with-hook
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
